title: Essential String methods
instruction:
String has a huge API. The ones you'll use every day:

- `.length()` — character count
- `.charAt(i)` — character at index i
- `.toUpperCase()` / `.toLowerCase()`
- `.trim()` — remove leading/trailing whitespace
- `.contains(str)` — does it contain this substring?
- `.startsWith(prefix)` / `.endsWith(suffix)`

Run this.
successMessage: Method chaining: s.trim().toUpperCase() — trim first, then uppercase. Strings are immutable — each method returns a NEW string, never modifying the original.

title: substring and indexOf
instruction:
Extract parts of a string:

- `.substring(start)` — from start to end
- `.substring(start, end)` — from start up to (not including) end
- `.indexOf(str)` — position of first match, -1 if not found
- `.lastIndexOf(str)` — position of last match

Parse a date string into its parts.
hint: date.substring(0, 4) for year, indexOf('-') for the separator position
successMessage: substring(0, 4) — start inclusive, end exclusive. indexOf('-') returns 4 — the position of the first dash. These two methods together parse almost any fixed-format string.

title: replace and split
instruction:
- `.replace(old, new)` — replace all occurrences (strings)
- `.replaceAll(regex, new)` — replace with regex pattern
- `.split(delimiter)` — split into array

Parse a CSV line and clean up a message.
expectedOutput: Learning Java is hard. Learning Java takes time.
successMessage: replace() swaps all occurrences. split() returns a String array — you can then iterate with for-each. CSV parsing is one of the most common real-world string tasks.

title: String.format — clean formatting
instruction:
`String.format()` is like printf from C — use placeholders:

- `%s` — String
- `%d` — integer
- `%f` — floating point
- `%.2f` — float with 2 decimal places
- `%n` — newline

Format a product receipt.
hint: String.format("%-12s $%6.2f", name, price) for aligned columns
successMessage: String.format is powerful for alignment. %-10s = left-align in 10 chars. %6.2f = right-align in 6 chars, 2 decimal places. This is how reports and log output are formatted.

title: equals vs == — the critical distinction
instruction:
This is Java's most common beginner trap:

- `==` compares **references** (are they the same object in memory?)
- `.equals()` compares **content** (do they contain the same characters?)

Always use `.equals()` for strings. Always.
successMessage: == is wrong for strings — don't use it. .equals() compares content and is always correct. .equalsIgnoreCase() for case-insensitive checks. This rule saves you from mysterious bugs.

title: StringBuilder — efficient string building
hint: StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); for each item: sb.append(item); if not last, sb.append(", ");
successMessage: StringBuilder appends in-place — no new objects created each iteration. For 1000 items, StringBuilder is orders of magnitude faster than string concatenation in a loop.

title: StringBuilder methods
instruction:
`StringBuilder` has more than just append:

- `.insert(index, str)` — insert at position
- `.delete(start, end)` — remove range
- `.reverse()` — reverse the string
- `.replace(start, end, str)` — replace range

Build and manipulate a greeting.
successMessage: StringBuilder is mutable — each operation modifies the same object. reverse() is often used in interview problems. reverse().toString().equals(original) checks if something is a palindrome.

title: Build a password validator
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write a `validatePassword(String pwd)` method that checks:
- At least 8 characters: `pwd.length() >= 8`
- Contains a digit: loop with `Character.isDigit(pwd.charAt(i))`
- Contains uppercase: `Character.isUpperCase(pwd.charAt(i))`
- Contains lowercase: `Character.isLowerCase(pwd.charAt(i))`

Print a report for three passwords: `"hello"`, `"Hello123"`, `"P@ssword1"`.
hint: Use a for loop with charAt(i) and Character.isDigit/isUpperCase/isLowerCase.

codeChecks.message: Use charAt(i) to check each character.

successMessage: A password validator using String and Character methods! charAt, isDigit, isUpperCase — this is how every sign-up form validates passwords. Chapter 4 done!
